perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall pay half (1/2) of the purchase price prior to shipment and the remaining balance 30 days after shipment of the Products. Payments received after the 30 day calendar period will be subject to a late fee of 1.5% of the invoice amount.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall pay half (1/2) of the purchase price prior to shipment and the remaining balance 60 days after shipment of the Products. Payments received after the 60 day calendar period will be subject to a late fee of 1.5% of the invoice amount.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 60 days after shipment could create issues under California's prompt payment laws if the distributor is considered a 'small business' as defined by state law and the agreement falls under the scope of those laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section dictates that if a home improvement contract does not comply with these provisions, then the contract is unenforceable against the homeowner. Allowing a 60 day payment period could conflict with the legal requirements for payment schedules, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period from 30 to 60 days, which could contradict California's prompt payment laws for small businesses. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this contradiction arises if the distributor qualifies as a 'small business' under California law, impacting the contract's enforceability. The Law Explanation indicates that non-compliance with legal payment schedule requirements could render the contract unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, highlights that this section applies to all home improvement contracts. Therefore, a 60-day payment period may violate the legal requirements for payment schedules, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the breach is not cured within ten days of such notice, the Agreement will terminate immediately.
perturbation.changed_text: If the breach is not cured within five days of such notice, the Agreement will terminate immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to five days for a material breach may be considered unconscionable or violate principles of good faith and fair dealing, particularly in California contract law, which emphasizes reasonable notice and opportunity to cure.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 deals with unconscionable contracts or clauses. A court could find that a five-day cure period is unreasonably short, particularly if the breach involves complex issues or requires significant effort to remedy, thus rendering the termination clause unconscionable and unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from ten to five days, which, as the perturbation explanation notes, could be deemed unconscionable, especially in California, due to potentially insufficient time to remedy the breach. The Law Explanation highlights California Civil Code § 1670.5, which addresses unconscionable contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes CIV 1670.5, stating that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. By reducing the cure period, the modified text introduces a clause that could be considered unconscionable, directly contradicting the principle outlined in the provided law snippet, thus the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Erchonia may revise its prices from time to time and deliver a written 90 day notice of the reasonable price revision to Distributor; provided that Erchonia agrees that the prices listed in Exhibit A shall not be increased during the first 90 days of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Erchonia may revise its prices from time to time and deliver a written 15 day notice of the reasonable price revision to Distributor; provided that Erchonia agrees that the prices listed in Exhibit A shall not be increased during the first 90 days of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the price revision notification period to 15 days may not provide sufficient time for the distributor to adjust its business operations, potentially conflicting with principles of good faith and fair dealing, especially under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly setting a specific timeframe, California Commercial Code § 2-305 deals with open price terms and requires that pricing adjustments be made in good faith. A 15-day notice period may be deemed unreasonable and not in good faith, especially if the distributor has made significant investments based on the initial pricing structure, potentially violating this provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2305 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2305. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) Nothing is said as to price; or (b) The price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) The price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as canceled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the price revision notification period from 90 days to 15 days, which the Law Explanation suggests may not be in good faith, potentially violating California Commercial Code § 2-305. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2, states in section 2305 (2): "A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith." The change in notification period could be interpreted as not allowing enough time for the distributor to reasonably adjust to the revised pricing, hence not in good faith, and contradicting the law.
